title: The Scribe
content:
What lovely things
    Thy hand hath made:
 The smooth-plumed bird
    In its emerald shade,
 The seed of the grass,
    The speck of stone
 Which the wayfaring ant
    Stirs - and hastes on!

 Though I should sit
    By some tarn in thy hills,
 Using its ink
    As the spirit wills
 To write of Earth's wonders,
    Its live, willed things,
 Flit would the ages
    On soundless wings.
 Ere unto Z
    My pen drew nigh;
 Leviathan told,
    And the honey-fly:
 And still would remain
    My wit to try
 My worn reeds broken,
    The dark tarn dry,
 All words forgotten - 
    Thou, Lord, and I.
meaning:
Walter De La Mare's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Scribe"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
